coredata.dc:title: 2D porous carbon nanosheet from sulfonated pitch-based graphene quantum dots for high volumetric performance EDLCs
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Panpan
Yang, Fan
Xie, Qingruo
Li, Tianhong
Dong, Jinshi
coredata.dc:description: With the ever-growing demand of miniaturized electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs), it is still a big challenge to well-balance the competing relationships between high specific surface area (SSA) and high packing density in porous carbons. Herein, 2D porous carbon nanosheet (PCN) with high carbon yield is synthesized by a simple interface self-assembly and subsequent activation strategy from industrial sulfonated pitch by-product, which is composed of graphene quantum dots. It is bestowed with ultrathin thickness of 20 nm, adequate SSA of 2429 m2 g−1, high packing density of 0.562 g cm−3, high e-conductivity of 89 S m−1 and high mechanical flexibility all in one, giving full play to the synergistic effect of electrons and ions. Based on the structure advantages, this PCN can be used as high-performance EDLC electrode. In pure EMIMBF4 electrolyte, it achieves high volumetric capacitances of 90.1 F cm−3 at 0.5 A g−1, excellent rate capability C20/0.05 of 71% and good high-voltage endurance lifetime of 88% after 5000 cycles at 1 A g−1. Satisfactorily, the maximum volumetric energy density can come up to 39 Wh L−1. More significantly, this work can also provide a new perspective on the architectural conception of other 2D carbon materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electric double-layer capacitors
Porous carbon nanosheet
Sulfonated pitch
Graphene quantum dots
Volumetric energy density

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and surface morphology of PCN1-800
XPS and elemental observations of SP and PCN1-800
Physical structures of SP and PCN1-800

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Scientific Research and Technology Development Program of Guangxi
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images (a, b) and TEM images (c, d) of PCN1-800.
C 1s (a) and O 1s (b) XPS spectra of PCN1-800.
XRD (a) and Raman spectra (b) of PCN1-800 (ID/IG was the intensity ratio of D band and G band).
Type I N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm of PCN1-800 (a), PSD curve and cumulative pore volume curve (b) calculated by NLDFT model of PCN1-800.

The CV curves (a) and GCD curves (b) of thin PCN1-800 electrode of 120 μm in pure EMIMBF4 electrolyte; the rate performance (c) of thin PCN1-800 electrode of 120 μm and thick PCN1-800 electrode of 200 μm in EMIMBF4 electrolyte; the life span (d) at 1 A g−1 of thin PCN1-800 electrode of 120 μm and thick PCN1-800 electrode of 200 μm in EMIMBF4 electrolyte; Nyquist plot (e) and the phase angle vs frequency plot (f) of thin PCN1-800 electrode of 120 μm in EMIMBF4 electrolyte. All tests were in two-electrode systems.

Ragone plots of thin PCN1-800 electrode of 120 μm and thick PCN1-800 electrode of 200 μm in TEABF4/PC and EMIMBF4 electrolytes, vs. PCN6@TEABF4/PC-2.7V [14], A1G@TEABF4/AN-2.7V [21], F310-800@TEABF4/PC-3V [34], HPGCS-600@TEABF4/AN-2.7V [44], GNa-CA@TEABF4/AN-2.7V [53], PCN6@EMIMBF4-3.5V [14], C-0.75-900@EMIMBF 4-4V [17], GPC-600@BMIMBF4/AN-3V [27], F310-800@EMIMBF4-3.5V [34], HN-DP–FSFC–2@BMIMBF4/AN-3V [36], HHCF@EMIMBF4-3.5V [42], OMCNS@EMIMBF4-3.5V [50], PCF-900@EMIMBF4-3.5V [54].
Graphic description of the preparation of PCN1-800.
Elemental analysis and surface atom analysis of SP and PCN1-800.
XRD parameters and e-conductivity of PCN1-800.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The e-conductivity was measured by the four-point probe method.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Pore structure parameters, carbon yield and packing density of PCN1-800.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: S mic = S BET - S mes; V mic = V tot - V mes.

head.ce:title: 2D porous carbon nanosheet from sulfonated pitch-based graphene quantum dots for high volumetric performance EDLCs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University of Science and Technology, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Green Processing of Sugar Resources, Key Laboratory for Processing of Sugar Resources of Guangxi Higher Education Institutes, Liuzhou, 545006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological and Chemical Engineering
Guangxi University of Science and Technology
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Green Processing of Sugar Resources
Key Laboratory for Processing of Sugar Resources of Guangxi Higher Education Institutes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University of Science and Technology, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Green Processing of Sugar Resources, Key Laboratory for Processing of Sugar Resources of Guangxi Higher Education Institutes, Liuzhou, 545006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the ever-growing demand of miniaturized electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs), it is still a big challenge to well-balance the competing relationships between high specific surface area (SSA) and high packing density in porous carbons. Herein, 2D porous carbon nanosheet (PCN) with high carbon yield is synthesized by a simple interface self-assembly and subsequent activation strategy from industrial sulfonated pitch by-product, which is composed of graphene quantum dots. It is bestowed with ultrathin thickness of 20 nm, adequate SSA of 2429 m2 g−1, high packing density of 0.562 g cm−3, high e-conductivity of 89 S m−1 and high mechanical flexibility all in one, giving full play to the synergistic effect of electrons and ions. Based on the structure advantages, this PCN can be used as high-performance EDLC electrode. In pure EMIMBF4 electrolyte, it achieves high volumetric capacitances of 90.1 F cm−3 at 0.5 A g−1, excellent rate capability C20/0.05 of 71% and good high-voltage endurance lifetime of 88% after 5000 cycles at 1 A g−1. Satisfactorily, the maximum volumetric energy density can come up to 39 Wh L−1. More significantly, this work can also provide a new perspective on the architectural conception of other 2D carbon materials.

ce:section.ce:para: With the virtues of high power density and long lifetime, electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) have evoked a great application foreground in energy field and urban rail transit system [1–3]. Recently, high requirements for the new generation of miniaturized and lightweight EDLCs have been put forward with the evergrowing demand of portable electronic devices [4,5]. The research core of EDLCs lies in the electrode materials, among which nano-porous carbon is the mainstream [6,7]. However, nano-porous carbons have several fatal drawbacks, such as low electron conductivity (e-conductivity, < 50 S m−1), inadequate ion-accessible specific surface area (SSA, < 2000 m2 g−1) and ultralow packing density (<0.4 g cm−3), which greatly limit the electrochemical characteristics of EDLCs (especially the volumetric energy density) [4,8].

Regarding this, several strategies are applied to well-design the architecture of porous carbons, which are mainly divided into two categories. One strategy is to construct graphene or carbon nanotubes (CNTs)-based porous carbons, rooting in their high intrinsic e-conductivity and efficient ion transport channels [9–11]. Derby's group fabricated a series of porous carbon aerogels from both CNTs and graphene with highly interconnected porous structure and ultrahigh e-conductivity of ~1000 S m−1. In view of the advantages, this material displays superior capacitive properties with gravimetric energy density (E g) of 21 Wh kg−1 in TEABF4/PC electrolyte [12]. Kim's group constructed a compact N-doped graphene microsphere with powder density as high as 0.91 g cm−3 and e-conductivity of 1500 S m−1. As EDLC electrodes, it exhibits ultrahigh volumetric energy density (E v) of 54 Wh L−1 in TEABF4/AN electrolyte [4]. Li's group synthesized a dense graphene-based thin-film with high bulk density of 1.33 g cm−3. The resultant graphene film-based device acquires remarkable E v of 59.9 Wh L−1 in EMIMBF4/AN electrolyte [13]. From the above results, graphene/CNTs-based porous carbons do indeed have excellent EDLC performances, but their large-scale application in EDLCs is largely restricted by the high cost of industrialization [14]. Therefore, it is still an urgent tendency to construct porous carbons from low-cost carbon precursors whose properties can be comparable to that of graphene and CNTs. Another strategy is to design porous carbons with unique 3D spatial morphology, namely 1D porous carbon fibers (PCFs), 2D porous carbon nanosheets (PCNs) and 3D hierarchical porous carbons (HPCs) [15–17]. Guo's group fabricated 1D heteroatoms-doped PCFs with high e-conductivity in axial direction and short ion transport distance in radial direction by means of electrospinning equipment. By right of high packing density of 1.0 g cm−3, this PCFs is equipped with high E v of 8.7 Wh L−1 in H2SO4 electrolyte [18]. Park's group constructed 1D P-doped porous carbon frameworks with hierarchical honeycomb structure from lignocellulosic biomass by steam activation. It exhibits high E g of 27.9 Wh kg−1 and stable cycle retention of 96% over 10,0000 cycles in NaClO4 electrolyte [19]. Zhang's group prepared 2D N-doped PCNs from phenylene diamine with ion-accessible SSA of 2406 m2 g−1 and applicable e-conductivity of 183 S m−1. The resultant flexible PCNs-based device presents high charge-storage capability with E v as high as 8.4 Wh L−1 in EMIMBF4 electrolyte [8]. Park's group fabricated 3D heteroatom-doped HPCs with high SSA of 1154 m2 g−1 by a one-pot hydrothermal method, possessing high E g of 8.4 Wh kg−1 and long cycle life in KOH electrolyte [20]. Chen's group constructed a 3D HPC with highly developed macro-mesoporous network and sufficient SSA of 3500 m2 g−1. However, the superabundant porosity makes this material relatively low packing density of 0.35 g cm−3, leading to low E v of only 13.7 Wh L−1 in TEABF4/AN electrolyte [21]. Summarily, the preparation of 1D PCFs mostly requires the peculiar electrospinning equipment, while the 3D HPCs with superfluous macropores often lead to low E v. Therefore, 2D PCNs is undoubtedly the first choice for high-performance EDLCs.

Around the architectural design of 2D PCNs, key breakthroughs have been witnessed over the past years. A simple synthetic strategy to construct PCNs is the one-step carbonization of small carbon-based molecules (for instance sodium gluconate, potassium citrate and so on) [22,23]. However, this strategy usually acquires low-down carbon yield (<15%), seriously limiting the actual utilization. Besides, another synthetic route to synthesize PCN is using vast biomass resources as carbon precursors, depending on their renewable ability and inherent porosity [1,7,24]. Unfortunately, most biomass-based PCNs can only achieve good electrochemical characteristics in aqueous electrolytes with voltage window less than 1.23 V, resulting in low E v [7,24].

To address the abovementioned issue, we demonstrate a new type of 2D PCN with high carbon yield (30%) through a simple interface self-assembly and subsequent activation strategy, which is derived from an industrialized by-product, namely sulfonated pitch (SP) with abundant π-conjugated sp2 carbon atoms [14]. This PCN is endowed with several structural advantages. i) Adequate ion-approachable SSA of 2429 m2 g−1 with micropores dominated. ii) Applicable packing density of 0.562 g cm−3 and robust mechanical ﬂexibility owing to without mesopores larger than 10 nm and macropores. iii) High e-conductivity of 89 S m−1 benefiting from the partly orientated carbonaceous microcrystallines and low oxygen-containing functional groups, guaranteeing the fast electron conduction. iv) Plentiful “shallow pores” with small ion transport distance (<10 nm) and low tortuosity thanks to the large aspect ratio and thin thickness of PCN, facilitating the rapid electrolyte ion transportation. Consequently, all the aforementioned advantages make this material great potential as a promising EDLC electrode material. When assembled into symmetric EDLC, this PCN owns high C g of 160.4 F g−1 and C v of 90.1 F cm−3 at 0.5 A g−1, good rate performance, synergistic high energy-power output ability (high E v of 39 Wh L−1) and prolonged lifetime in EMIMBF4 electrolyte.

SP was supplied by Shandong long-quan chemical plant in China and purified by deionized water to remove the impurity for further use. Triblock copolymer P123, absolute ethanol, KOH and HCl were all purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. LTD without further treatment. PTFE (65 wt% dispersion), cellulose paper (Celgard 2400), TEABF4/PC and EMIMBF4 were used as received from Suzhou Dodochem technology Co. LTD.

Typically, 2 g SP, 1/2/3 g P123 and 6 g KOH were joined into 200 mL deionized water with stirring for 3 h at 30 °C until fully mixed. Next, 10 mL absolute ethanol was added into the above mixture and the stirring was proceeding for 24 h at 90 °C for the interface self-assembly between SP and P123. After thoroughly drying, the obtained intermediate samples were activated at 800 °C for 1 h at 2 °C min−1 for the conventional KOH activation under N2. The samples were acquired after fully washing with HCl and deionized water. And then the samples were further treated by high-temperature annealing up to 800 °C at 2 °C min−1 and the following natural cooling under N2. At last, the final products named as PCN0.5-800, PCN1-800 and PCN1.5-800 were got, in which the number 0.5, 1 and 1.5 represent the mass ratio of P123 to SP and the number 800 means the annealing temperature.

The surface morphologies and microstructures of material were manifested by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Philips Nano 430) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips Tecnai G2 F20). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to characterize the carbonaceous microcrystallines on Rigaku D/Max 2500 v/PC instrument by employing Cu Kα radiation (40 kV, 200 mA, λ = 1.54051 Å). Raman spectra were recorded on Renishaw MKI-2000 spectrometer to analyze the crystal and defective nature of carbon material with wavelength of 532 nm. The pore structure was determined by the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms on Micrometrics ASAP 2020 system at 77 K. The SSA and pore size distribution (PSD) were respectively calculated by the BET method and non-local density functional theory (NLDFT). The total pore volume (V tot) was measured at a relative pressure P/P 0 of 0.995. Elemental analysis was conducted on Vario EL Ⅲ elemental setup. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried on PHI-1600 ESCA electron setup using Al Kα (1486.6 eV) radiation. The e-conductivity of material was measured by RTS-9 type four-point probe equipment.

The electrochemical measurements in this work used the most credible industrial testing method raised by Gogotsi's group to establish better data reliability, namely the two-electrode symmetrical system (CR2032-type coin cells) [25,26]. In order to construct the electrode, the PCN powder (90 wt%) was firstly blended with PTFE binder (10 wt%) in ethanol solvent without any conductive agent. And then the slurry was rolled into thin film of 120 μm and thick film of 200 μm, pressed onto the carbon-coated aluminum foil and cut into circular electrode with 13 mm in diameter. The active material mass loading (m areal) in thin electrode of 120 μm was 6.33 mg cm−2, while the m areal in thick electrode of 200 μm are 10.47 mg cm−2. Cellulose paper was used as the separator, while 1 M TEABF4/PC and pure EMIMBF4 were served as electrolytes. After drying, a pair of electrodes was assembled into CR2032-type coin cell in a pure Ar glove box. Galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) tests were carried out by Arbin battery test apparatus with current density ranging from 0.05 to 20 A g−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was tested under the open circuit voltage between 0.1 Hz and 100 kHz. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was tested with scan rate of 20 mV s−1 to 400 mV s−1. Both EIS and CV tests were carried out by CHI760E electrochemical workstation. The potential range in TEABF4/PC was 0–2.7 V and that in EMIMBF4 was 0–3.5 V. As for porous carbons without macropores and intergranular voids, the packing density (ρ) of material can be theoretically estimated by the following equation of ρ (g cm−3) = (V tot+1/ρ
T )−1, in which V tot (cm3 g−1) is the total pore volume from the N2 adsorption testing and ρ
T (g cm−3) is the true density of carbon (2.0 g cm−3) [4,27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and surface morphology of PCN1-800
ce:section.ce:para: SEM and TEM images in Fig. 1 shed light on the surface topography and microstructures of PCN1-800. It has a unique 2D architecture with slightly curved surface and outstanding ﬂexibility (Fig. 1a&b), accompanied by large planar size (>5 μm) and ultrathin thickness (<20 nm). This statement is also confirmed by the TEM inspection with high-degree of transparency in Fig. 1c. Simultaneously, there are a certain number of turbostratic and short-range ordered graphitic domains (highlighted by yellow arrows in Fig. 1d), guaranteeing excellent e-conductivity and partial graphitization of PCN1-800 (proved by the XPS and Raman results) [36]. These graphitic domains are not only derived from the thermal treatment, but also from the inborn π-conjugated hexatomic rings of GQDs in SP carbon precursor [14,37]. Furthermore, there exist plentiful micropores and small mesopores (<10 nm) inside the nanosheets (highlighted by white circles in Fig. 1d), ensuring high SSA of PCN1-800 (verified by the N2 adsorption results). Approvingly, the scarcely any larger mesopores (>6 nm) and macropores allow high packing density of PCN1-800. In brief, the typical 2D network can guarantee that the electron conduction occurs within the plane and the ion transport is perpendicular to the plane, giving full play to the synergistic effect of electrons and ions (shown in Scheme 1). Hence, by virtue of the above peculiar advantages, PCN1-800 displays good prospect in EDLC field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS and elemental observations of SP and PCN1-800
ce:section.ce:para: The elemental and surface atom analyses of SP and PCN1-800 are summarized in Table 1. After activation, both the bulk and surface oxygen contents decline sharply. The C content is as high as 88.78 wt% and O content is only 9.03 wt%, together with the increase of C/O ratio from 3.2 of SP to 10.2 of PCN1-800. C 1s and O 1s XPS spectra of SP and PCN1-800 (Fig. 2 and Fig. S2) explicitly expound the chemical state of C and O atoms. The C 1s peaks located at 284.6 eV, 285.3 eV can be respectively assigned to the predominant sp2-bonding carbons and the disadvantaged sp3-bonding carbons (Fig. 2a) [6,30]. By calculating the peak area ratio of sp2 carbon to the sum of sp2 carbon and sp3 carbon, the sp2-bonding carbon content in PCN1-800 can be roughly estimated to be 89%, much larger than that in SP (71%) [10,34,38]. This further substantiates that upon annealing, the sp3 carbon in the form of aliphatic hydrocarbon from SP can be transformed into sp2 graphitic domains through the aromatization, fitting well with the Raman analysis [30,33]. Three extremely weak peaks at higher binding energy are respectively representative of -C-O group (286.3 eV), –CO group (288 eV) and –COOH (289 eV), showing low levels of oxygen content in PCN1-800. It stays closely aligned with the O 1s spectrum. Three peaks located at 532 eV, 533 eV and 535 eV can be classified as –CO group (carbonyl or quinone), -C-O group (ether) and –COOH (Fig. 2b) [5,39]. It is demonstrated that the stabilities of –CO and -C-O groups are much higher than that of –COOH group at high voltage in organic electrolyte [40]. In short, thanks to the high sp2 carbon content, high C/O atom ratio and ultralow content of unstable –COOH (23%), the resultant PCN1-800 electrode can be equipped with high e-conductivity and long cycle life.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical structures of SP and PCN1-800
ce:section.ce:para: The crystalline structures of carbon precursor SP and PCN1-800 are investigated by XRD spectra in Fig. S1 and Fig. 3 a. The two dispersive peaks located at around 25° and 43° are respectively recognized as 002 and 100 diffraction peaks of carbon materials, suggesting the short-range ordered amorphous structure [7,11]. Owing to the KOH activation, compared with the carbon precursor SP, the 002 peak of PCN1-800 pronouncedly becomes broader and moves to lower angle of 24.06°, along with the increase of average interlayer spacing d 002 from 0.3437 nm of SP to 0.3696 nm of PCN1-800 (Table 2). Furthermore, the 100 peak of PCN1-800 slightly becomes sharper than that of SP, highlighting the larger planar aromatic size L a of PCN1-800 (5.90 nm) than that of SP (3.2 nm). This corresponds to the formation of C–C covalent bonds between contiguous GQDs and the aromatization of carbon materials at high temperature (consistent with the TEM observation) [33,34]. In addition, the obvious diffraction region at low angle indicates that there exist masses of micropores, which reaches agreement with the N2 adsorption results and TEM images.

The carbonaceous microcrystalline of PCN1-800 is also evaluated by Raman spectrum in Fig. 3b. The two protruding peaks located at 1598 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1 refer to the G band (the first-order scattering of E 2g mode for ordered graphitic structure) and D band (the defects for disordered carbon) of carbon materials, respectively [41,42]. The ID/IG value of PCN1-800 is 1.51, less than many reported values in literature [6,34,43]. This is indicative of the relatively higher graphitized degree of PCN1-800 than other porous carbons, referable to the formation of oriented graphitic crystallites upon annealing (proved by the TEM and XPS observations) [44]. As a consequence, the e-conductivity of PCN1-800 is assessed to be as high as 89 S m−1. Benefiting from the remarkable e-conductivity, it is noteworthy that PCN1-800 can be directly used as EDLC electrode without any addition of conductive agent [39]. What's more, PCN1-800 can be predictably endowed with high power-output capability and long lifespan.

Notably, the V tot of PCN1-800 is 1.28 cm3 g−1. Thus, the packing density is determined to be 0.562 g cm−3 (Table 3). On account of the inborn π-conjugated moieties in SP, the carbon yield of PCN1-800 is as high as 30%, much higher than other porous carbons (~15%) from small carbon-based molecules or biomass [1,27,41,46–48]. Meanwhile, the SSA of PCN1-800 is 2429 m2 g−1, with S mic /S BET as high as 76.9% (Table 3). As previously mentioned, PCN1-800 has typical 2D structure with ultrathin thickness of 20 nm. It is worth noting that all the pores can be identified as “shallow pores” with ion transport distance less than 10 nm. Therefore, these “shallow pores” can render PCN1-800 abundant ion-approachable SSA and unblocked channels for ion storage and diffusion, beneficial to high C v and good rate capability [8,37].

In virtue of the well-balanced high e-conductivity, packing density and SSA with the combination of low oxygen content and robust ﬂexibility, PCN1-800 can be endowed with high energy-storage characteristics for EDLC. As a proof-of-concept, PCN1-800 was prepared into EDLC electrode totally free of any conductive additive [39]. To insure the credible data, all electrochemical properties were tested on the two-electrode coin cell, using TEABF4/PC and EMIMBF4 as electrolytes [21,25].

Firstly, CV and GCD curves were implemented to probe into the specific capacity and rate performance with voltage window of 0–3.5 V in EMIMBF4 electrolyte. CV plots (Fig. 5 a) present quasi-rectangular shapes, indicating the symbolic EDLC behaviors of thin PCN1-800 electrode. Even though the scanning rate rises to 400 mV s−1, the rectangular shape is still well-maintained without apparent distortion. It offers clue of fine reversibility and high rate capability for thin PCN1-800 electrode, proved by the highly symmetric triangular GCD curves even as the current density up to 20 A g−1 (Fig. 5b). The calculated C g of thin PCN1-800 electrode at 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20 A g−1 are respectively 160.4, 157.4, 141, 126.2, 115.6 F g−1, accompanied by capacity retention C20/0.05 of 71%. Based on the packing density of 0.562 g cm−3, the C v of thin PCN1-800 electrode are respectively 90.1, 88.5, 79.1, 71 F cm−3 at 0.5, 1, 5, 10 A g−1 (Fig. 5c), which is superior to many reported porous carbons (shown in Table S1&2) [14,17,21,34,36,42,44]. And with the increase of mass loading, the C v of thick PCN1-800 electrode at 0.5, 1, 5, 10 A g−1 are respectively 85.1, 82.9, 71.5, 63.6 F cm−3, with lower capacity retention C10/0.05 of 69.5% (Fig. 5c). The fundamental reason for high C v and rate capability of PCN1-800 is the synergistic relationship between ample “shallow pores”, thin thickness and few larger pores. Besides, The C v of thin PCN1-800 electrode (Fig. S7e) in TEABF4/PC electrolyte are 82, 75.3, 69.1 F cm−3 at 1, 5, 10 A g−1. As is well-known, pure EMIMBF4 electrolyte is often faced with higher viscosity and lower ion-mobility than TEABF4/PC electrolyte, leading to poor rate capability. Satisfactorily, the EDLC performances of PCN1-800 in EMIMBF4 precede that in TEABF4/PC (Fig. S7). It is attributed to the unique 2D structure of PCN1-800 with facilely ion-reachable external and internal surface. Furthermore, the thin PCN1-800 electrode and thick PCN1-800 electrode respectively acquire high capacitance retention of 88% and 85.7% after 5000 cycles at 1 A g−1 (Fig. 5d). The good lifespan of PCN1-800 is directly related to the appropriate e-conductivity and low-levels of oxygen-containing configurations (Table 1), largely restricting the unwelcome side reactions [34,40].

Nyquist plot (Fig. 5e) provides deeper insights to the charge-transfer and ion-migration peculiarities of thin PCN1-800 electrode in EMIMBF4 electrolyte. The x-intercept at high frequency is only 0.73 Ω, illustrating the diminutive equivalent series resistance (R S) [49]. This is directly relevant to the excellent e-conductivity, derived from the continuous carbon network skeleton of material. The semicircle at high frequency signifies the charge transfer resistance (R ct), composed of the electron conduction among the particle interface and ion transport from bulk electrolyte to outside surface of electrode [39,50]. The R ct is only 0.55 Ω. This stems from the typical 2D architecture, ensuing the fine ion-wettability and instantaneous contact between the electrolyte ion and outer surface of material. At medium frequency, the 45° oblique line represents Warburg resistance, namely the ion permeation and diffusion resistance inside the electrode. As mentioned earlier, all the pores are of low tortuosity with maximum ion-transmission distance of ~10 nm (confirmed by the SEM and N2 adsorption results). Thus, the quick ion-transfer speed vests PCN1-800 electrode small Warburg resistance of 1.03 Ω, much smaller than the values reported elsewhere [8,34]. The phase angle at minimum frequency of 0.01 Hz is 83°, manifesting near-ideal EDLC behavior of PCN1-800 electrode (Fig. 5f). It is also proved by the nearly vertical line at low-frequency region. More importantly, the corresponding frequency at 45° phase angle can be used to calculate the relaxation time constant (τ 0), which means the speed of reversible running of EDLC [17,37,51]. The τ 0 value is merely 3.1 s, testifying the wonderful ion-transport kinetics of PCN1-800 electrode. In brief, the low Warburg resistance and small τ 0 value make PCN1-800 electrode high rate capability, alignment with the GCD and CV results.

The integrated alignments between high C v, high rate capability and small τ 0 together boost PCN1-800 electrode with both high energy density and power density, elaborated by Ragone plots in Fig. 6 . In TEABF4/PC electrolyte, the E g of thin PCN1-800 electrode are 38.6 Wh kg−1 at gravimetric power density (P g) of 33.75 W kg−1 and 31.2 Wh kg−1 at high P g of 6.75 kW kg−1 (Fig. S8), respectively. According to the packing density (0.562 g cm−3), the E v of thin PCN1-800 electrode is 17.56 Wh L−1 at high volumetric power density (P v) of 3.8 kW L−1. And with the increase of mass loading, at the same P v of 3.8 kW L−1, the E v of thick PCN1-800 electrode is 15.53 Wh L−1. Based on the equation of E = 1/2CV2, the energy density can be substantially improved by higher voltage window of 3.5 V in EMIMBF4 electrolyte. Thus, the thin PCN1-800 electrode can attain high E g of 69.35 Wh kg−1 at P g of 43.75 W kg−1 and still maintain E g of 49.4 Wh kg−1 at ultrahigh P g of 17.5 kW kg−1 (Fig. S8). It is noteworthy that the E v of thin PCN1-800 electrode can be as high as 39 Wh L−1 at P v of 24.6 W L−1, while at high P v of 9.88 kW L−1, the E v can still keep 27.75 Wh L−1. And with the increase of mass loading, at high P v of 4.94 kW L−1, the E v of thick PCN1-800 electrode is 27.2 Wh L−1. These values of PCN1-800 markedly borne comparison with or even exceed many advanced porous carbons in literature, such as graphene/CNTs-based porous carbons [10,27,51,52], PCNs [14,22,44,50,53], HPCs [17,21,34,36,42,54–56] and porous carbon sphere/aerogels [57,58], displayed in Fig. 6 and Table S2&3.

To sum up, the newfangled 2D structure, adequate SSA, high packing density, appropriate e-conductivity and ultrathin thickness in union bestow PCN1-800 high energy-storage abilities, for instance high C v and E v, good rate performance and long lifetime. The prominent multi-advantages are generalized as follows: (1) the typical 2D structure with fine interfacial affinity solves the external diffusion problem of electrolyte ions from the liquid phase to the solid phase surface of electrode; (2) ample “shallow pores” with short ion-transfer distance (<10 nm) solves the internal diffusion problem of electrolyte ions from the outer-surface into the inner pores of electrode without retardation; (3) the high sp2 carbon content and low oxygen content enable high e-conductivity, so as to ensure the rapid electron-conduction and thus good high-voltage endurance capacity of electrode; (4) high packing density and robust ﬂexibility meet the requirements of high volumetric energy and power density for miniaturized EDLC device.

In summary, we proposed a facile interface self-assembly, KOH activation and subsequent annealing strategy to construct the 2D PCN, using SP as carbon source and P123 as soft-template. Different from other porous carbons with low carbon yield, this PCN obtains high carbon yield of 30%, attributed to the inherent π-conjugated hexatomic rings of GQDs in SP. Meanwhile, it is empowered with ultrathin thickness, abundant ion-accessible SSA, high sp2 carbon content, high e-conductivity, dense bulk density and strong flexibility. Such peculiar structural advantages afford this PCN numerous ion-adsorption sites and ion-transport channels, exemplifying the remarkable potential for EDLC. Even absolutely free of any conductive agent, this PCN-based electrode can not only harvest high specific capacitance and good rate capability, but also small τ 0 and good high-voltage endurance cycling stability. In EMIMBF4 electrolyte, the E v can be as high as 27.75 Wh L−1 at ultrahigh P v of 9.88 kW L−1, superior to many progressive nanoporous carbons and commercial activated carbon. Thus this PCN can be gifted with more widespread applications, such as Li–S battery, electro-catalysis field and so on. More significantly, this work can provide a new perspective on the high value-added utilization of industrial by-product SP.

Panpan Chang: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Fan Yang: Experiment, Data processing, Writing - original draft, Formal analysis. Qingruo Xie: Writing - review & editing, Investigation. Tianhong Li: Experiment, Investigation. Jinshi Dong: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21905061), the Scientific Research and Technology Development Program of Guangxi (Special talent, GUIKEAD19110035, GUIKEAD19110103) and the Doctoral Fund Program of Guangxi University of Science and Technology (18Z15).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Tang, J. Wang, L.K. Shrestha, M.S.A. Hossain, Z.A. Alothman, Y. Yamauchi, K. Ariga, Activated Porous Carbon Spheres with Customized Mesopores through Assembly of Diblock Copolymers for Electrochemical Capacitor, ACS applied materials & interfaces 9(22) (2017) 18986-18993.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbide-derived carbon aerogels with tunable pore structure as versatile electrode material in high power supercapacitors
Carbon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Oschatz, S. Boukhalfa, W. Nickel, J.P. Hofmann, C. Fischer, G. Yushin, S. Kaskel, Carbide-derived carbon aerogels with tunable pore structure as versatile electrode material in high power supercapacitors, Carbon 113 (2017) 283-291.
